questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy presents with a 2-day history of fever, purulent nasal discharge, and periorbital swelling. On examination, there is proptosis, conjunctival chemosis, and restricted eye movements in all directions. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of fever, sinusitis, proptosis, and ophthalmoplegia indicates infection posterior to the orbital septum, i.e., orbital cellulitis. Preseptal cellulitis lacks proptosis and eye movement restriction; cavernous sinus thrombosis is bilateral and includes headache; mucormycosis typically occurs in immunocompromised patients with eschar; pseudotumor is afebrile.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with poorly controlled diabetes and recent prednisolone use for COPD presents with periorbital swelling, nasal congestion, and a black eschar on the hard palate. He is afebrile. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The black eschar and immunocompromised state (diabetes, steroids) are classic for invasive fungal rhinosinusitis due to Mucorales. Orbital cellulitis typically presents with fever and purulence; cavernous sinus thrombosis is bilateral with fever; allergic fungal sinusitis is chronic and non-invasive.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old girl presents with periorbital erythema and swelling of the left eye, mild fever, and no pain on eye movement. There is no proptosis, and vision is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The absence of proptosis and ophthalmoplegia localises the infection anterior to the orbital septum, consistent with preseptal cellulitis. Dacryocystitis involves the lacrimal sac with medial canthus swelling; allergic oedema is typically bilateral and afebrile; pseudotumor causes pain and may have proptosis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with acute sinusitis develops high fever, severe headache, bilateral periorbital oedema, proptosis, and ptosis. There is limited eye movement in all directions. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Bilateral involvement, severe headache, and cranial nerve palsy (ptosis) point to cavernous sinus thrombosis. Bilateral orbital cellulitis is rare; mucormycosis typically has eschar; meningitis has neck stiffness; carotid-cavernous fistula may cause pulsatile proptosis and bruit.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with painful, red, swollen left eye and mild proptosis. She is afebrile, has no sinusitis, and has not responded to a course of co-amoxiclav. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Afebrile presentation with no response to antibiotics suggests idiopathic orbital inflammation (pseudotumor). Thyroid eye disease is usually bilateral with lid retraction and often gradual onset; dacryoadenitis affects the lacrimal gland; orbital cellulitis would have fever and purulent sinusitis.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a 2-day history of fever, right periorbital swelling, and erythema. On examination, he has proptosis, limitation of eye movements, and pain on eye movement. He is systemically unwell. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Plain X-ray of the facial bones
questions.explanation: CT orbits with contrast is the gold standard for diagnosing orbital cellulitis, as it can assess the extent of infection, identify abscess formation, and differentiate from preseptal cellulitis. MRI is useful for intracranial extension but is not the first-line.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with a history of sinusitis presents with left periorbital swelling and fever. On examination, there is no proptosis, no ophthalmoplegia, and visual acuity is normal. What clinical feature would be most indicative of orbital involvement rather than preseptal cellulitis?
questions.explanation: Pain on eye movement suggests inflammation or infection of the extraocular muscles within the orbit, which is a key distinguishing feature of orbital cellulitis. Preseptal cellulitis typically presents with swelling and erythema anterior to the orbital septum without ocular motility issues.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with orbital cellulitis develops sudden deterioration with headache, photophobia, and sixth nerve palsy. There is suspicion of cavernous sinus thrombosis. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm this complication?
questions.explanation: MR venography is the best non-invasive test to confirm cavernous sinus thrombosis, as it directly visualizes the venous sinuses. CT venogram is an alternative but MRV avoids radiation and provides superior soft tissue detail.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old girl with orbital cellulitis fails to improve after 48 hours of intravenous antibiotics. Clinically, she remains febrile with worsening proptosis and ophthalmoplegia. What is the most appropriate next step to guide surgical intervention?
questions.explanation: CT orbits with contrast is the investigation of choice to identify a subperiosteal abscess requiring surgical drainage. It provides detailed bony and soft tissue assessment of the orbit and sinuses.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child presents with acute onset of right periorbital swelling, fever, and mild conjunctival injection. There is no proptosis and eye movements are full. What is the most likely diagnosis based on these clinical features?
questions.explanation: The absence of proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and pain on eye movement makes orbital involvement unlikely. Preseptal cellulitis typically presents with periorbital swelling and erythema without signs of orbital inflammation.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old child presents with 2-day history of periorbital redness, swelling, fever, and mild proptosis. There is no visual loss or ophthalmoplegia. CT shows preseptal and early postseptal inflammation. What is the most appropriate first-line IV antibiotic therapy?
questions.explanation: Co-amoxiclav is first-line for mild-to-moderate orbital cellulitis in children, covering common pathogens (Staph, Strep, anaerobes). Ceftriaxone is reserved for severe cases; vancomycin for MRSA; metronidazole lacks Gram-positive cover; oral therapy is insufficient.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known sinusitis presents with acute onset right eye pain, proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and reduced visual acuity. CT confirms orbital cellulitis with a subperiosteal abscess. What is the most appropriate immediate medical management?
questions.explanation: Severe orbital cellulitis with abscess requires broad-spectrum IV antibiotics including anaerobic cover; ceftriaxone + metronidazole achieves this. Co-amoxiclav is for milder cases; vancomycin is unnecessary without MRSA risk; surgery is adjunctive; ceftriaxone alone misses anaerobes.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with poorly controlled diabetes develops orbital cellulitis. She then develops high fever, headache, and ipsilateral cranial nerve palsies suggestive of cavernous sinus thrombosis. What definitive therapy should be added?
questions.explanation: Cavernous sinus thrombosis in orbital cellulitis is typically due to anaerobic extension from sinuses; adding metronidazole provides essential anaerobic cover. Acyclovir is for viral; heparin is controversial; piperacillin-tazobactam could be an alternative but the question asks 'add'; fluconazole is not indicated.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old intravenous drug user presents with orbital cellulitis. Blood cultures grow methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA). What is the most appropriate antibiotic?
questions.explanation: Flucloxacillin is first-line for MSSA, providing narrow-spectrum coverage. Vancomycin and teicoplanin are reserved for MRSA; ceftriaxone is less effective against Staph; clindamycin is an alternative but not first-line.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old patient with known immediate-type penicillin anaphylaxis develops mild orbital cellulitis (no vision loss, no abscess). What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?
questions.explanation: In penicillin anaphylaxis, avoid beta-lactams. Clindamycin covers Gram-positives; ciprofloxacin covers Gram-negatives (including H. influenzae). Vancomycin alone misses Gram-negatives; ceftriaxone is cross-reactive; doxycycline is not first-line; oral therapy is insufficient.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old child presents with a 2-day history of right periorbital swelling, redness, and mild fever. On examination, there is eyelid oedema and erythema without proptosis or ophthalmoplegia. Visual acuity is normal. Temperature 38.2°C. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange urgent CT orbits and sinuses
Obtain blood cultures and start intravenous antibiotics
questions.explanation: This is likely preseptal cellulitis given the absence of proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, or vision loss. NICE guidelines recommend oral antibiotics (co-amoxiclav) as first-line for mild/moderate preseptal cellulitis. Intravenous antibiotics are reserved for severe cases or orbital involvement. Imaging is not routinely needed for preseptal cellulitis.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with a history of sinusitis presents with 2 days of left periorbital swelling, proptosis, and painful eye movements. He has a temperature of 39.0°C and left eye vision reduced to 6/12. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Request urgent CT orbits and sinuses
Apply warm compresses and advise analgesia
Arrange urgent ophthalmology and ENT review
Take blood cultures and start intravenous aciclovir
questions.explanation: This presentation suggests orbital cellulitis with visual compromise. Immediate administration of intravenous antibiotics (co-amoxiclav is first-line) should not be delayed by imaging or other investigations. CT can be arranged concurrently but antibiotics are the priority. Option 2 is reasonable but not the immediate action; option 5 is necessary but secondary.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old female with orbital cellulitis is on intravenous co-amoxiclav. After 48 hours, her proptosis has worsened, and she has developed a relative afferent pupillary defect and fixed, dilated pupil on the affected side. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform a lumbar puncture to rule out meningitis
Add empiric vancomycin to the antibiotic regimen
Arrange urgent surgical decompression and drainage
questions.explanation: The development of a relative afferent pupillary defect and fixed dilated pupil indicates optic nerve compression, likely from an orbital abscess. Urgent surgical drainage (via external ethmoidectomy or orbital decompression) is required to prevent permanent vision loss. CT may be done if time allows, but clinical deterioration mandates immediate surgical intervention.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old diabetic man presents with severe right periorbital swelling, nasal congestion, and dark nasal discharge. He is afebrile. Examination reveals periorbital cellulitis with slight proptosis but normal eye movements and vision. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: This presentation in a diabetic patient raises concern for invasive fungal sinusitis (e.g., mucormycosis), which can mimic orbital cellulitis. Urgent CT is essential to assess for bony erosion and sinus involvement. Nasal endoscopy and biopsy are the definitive diagnostic step but require ENT involvement and may follow imaging. Empirical antifungal therapy should not be started without tissue diagnosis unless highly suspicious. Antibiotics alone are insufficient.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with known orbital cellulitis on intravenous antibiotics develops sudden severe headache, photophobia, and diplopia. Examination reveals bilateral eyelid oedema, proptosis, and a right third nerve palsy. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange urgent MRI brain with gadolinium
questions.explanation: The clinical picture suggests cavernous sinus thrombosis (CST), a severe complication. Urgent CT venogram (or MR venogram) is the next step to confirm the diagnosis. Anticoagulation (heparin) is controversial and should only be started after imaging confirms CST, as it carries risk of haemorrhage. Lumbar puncture may be considered if meningitis is suspected, but imaging takes priority.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old child presents with 2-day history of right periorbital swelling, fever, and nasal congestion. On examination, there is erythema and edema of the right eyelids, conjunctival injection, and the child complains of pain when moving the right eye. Which single clinical finding would most strongly indicate that this is orbital cellulitis (and not preseptal cellulitis)?
questions.explanation: Proptosis indicates posterior extension beyond the orbital septum into the orbit, distinguishing orbital from preseptal cellulitis. Chemosis and fever can occur in preseptal cellulitis, purulent discharge suggests conjunctivitis, and CRP is non-specific.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with a history of recurrent sinusitis presents with left periorbital swelling and mild eyelid erythema for 24 hours. She is afebrile and has no visual symptoms. Which finding on examination would warrant urgent CT imaging to rule out orbital involvement?
questions.explanation: Pain or limitation of eye movement suggests extraocular muscle involvement, a hallmark of orbital cellulitis and an indication for CT. The other findings are consistent with sinusitis or preseptal cellulitis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with severe right orbital cellulitis is being treated with intravenous antibiotics. He develops sudden-onset bilateral periorbital swelling and worsening headache. Which sign, if present, would indicate the life-threatening complication of cavernous sinus thrombosis?
options: Cranial nerve VI palsy on the right
questions.explanation: Cavernous sinus thrombosis involves the cavernous sinus, affecting cranial nerves III, IV, V1, V2, and VI. CN VI is especially vulnerable due to its free course within the sinus, and its palsy is an early sign. Bilateral chemosis can occur but is less specific; unilateral proptosis is present in orbital cellulitis itself.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old diabetic patient with orbital cellulitis complains of sudden loss of vision in the affected eye. Which clinical finding most strongly suggests optic nerve compression requiring urgent surgical decompression?
questions.explanation: RAPD is an objective sign of optic nerve dysfunction, indicating compression that may lead to irreversible vision loss if not decompressed emergently. Proptosis and edema are common, and diplopia may occur without optic nerve involvement.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old poorly controlled diabetic man with bilateral periorbital swelling and fever is suspected of having orbital cellulitis. He is not improving on broad-spectrum antibiotics. Which finding on examination should raise suspicion for rhinocerebral mucormycosis rather than bacterial orbital cellulitis?
questions.explanation: Mucormycosis is an angioinvasive fungal infection that causes tissue necrosis, often presenting with a black eschar on the nasal turbinates or palate. This is a classic distinguishing feature from bacterial cellulitis. Proptosis, chemosis, cranial neuropathies, and RAPD can occur in both, but the eschar is pathognomonic.
